parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I’ve come to the conclusion that I’m just naturally bracing for disappointment in every interaction lately. It’s like a sudden switch flipped a few days ago, and now I’m reading into every little pause or short reply. I catch myself overthinking messages that probably mean absolutely nothing at all. It’s exhausting trying to keep my emotional walls up all the time when I really just want to relax.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly draining. It’s tough when your mind starts interpreting even neutral things as signs of something negative—almost like your emotional guard is stuck in overdrive. That switch you mentioned can feel disorienting, especially when it comes out of nowhere.

Sometimes our brains do this as a way of trying to protect us, especially if there’s been past hurt or stress. But when it becomes constant, it can feel isolating and exhausting, like you’re never really present in the moment.

Would it help to talk about what might have triggered this shift a few days ago? Or would you rather just sit with this feeling for a bit without digging into causes? Either way, I’m here to listen.

turns.user_message: This defensive habit has completely taken over my daily routine now. I find myself drafting and deleting replies for hours because I’m terrified of saying the wrong thing. Every interaction feels like walking on thin ice, and I’m constantly bracing for some kind of quiet rejection. It’s frustrating because I know I’m pushing people away before they even get the chance to step closer.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly exhausting and isolating. It’s like you’re caught in a loop where the fear of being misunderstood or rejected ends up creating the very distance you’re trying to avoid. That kind of hyper-vigilance can drain so much mental and emotional energy.

It’s brave of you to recognize this pattern—it’s the first step toward shifting it. You’re not alone in feeling this way. Many people struggle with similar fears, especially when they deeply care about how they’re perceived.

Would it help to explore where this fear might be coming from? Sometimes, past experiences or certain beliefs we hold about ourselves can fuel these defensive habits. Or would you prefer to talk about small, practical ways to ease the pressure in your interactions?

turns.user_message: I honestly don’t recognize the person I’ve become lately, and it’s starting to weigh heavily on my chest. I’m so convinced that I’m going to mess everything up that I’ve stopped reaching out entirely. My mind spins in endless loops, replaying every awkward moment and convincing myself I’m fundamentally unapproachable. I just feel completely trapped inside my own head, watching connections slip through my fingers because I’m too scared to just be myself.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly heavy to carry, and I want you to know that what you’re feeling is deeply human. It takes a lot of courage to even put words to that kind of internal struggle.

It’s like you’re watching yourself from a distance, and the version of you that you see feels unfamiliar and stuck. That sense of being trapped in your own thoughts—especially when they’re looping on past moments or fears of future mistakes—can be exhausting and isolating.

You mentioned being scared to be yourself. I wonder if, in trying to protect yourself from messing things up, you’ve put a version of you in a kind of emotional quarantine. It’s a painful place to be, because the longing for connection is still there, but the fear feels louder right now.

Would it feel okay to gently challenge the idea that you’re “fundamentally unapproachable”? Sometimes our minds tell us stories that feel true in the moment, but they aren’t the whole truth about who we are. The fact that you care so much about connection—that you’re even aware of this tension—says something meaningful about your capacity for empathy and self-reflection.

Is there one small, low-pressure way you could practice reaching out again? Maybe sending a text to someone you trust, or even just acknowledging how you’re feeling to yourself without judgment? Sometimes reconnecting starts with tiny, gentle steps—not to “fix” everything at once, but to remind yourself that you’re still here, and you’re allowed to take up space.

You don’t have to have all the answers right now. Just know that it’s okay to feel this way, and it’s also okay to want something different for yourself. You’re not alone in this.
